item.title: Blue Prince on Nintendo Switch 2, Mixtape, Ratatan Release Date, and More: Indie World Showcase Round-Up
item.content:encoded:
Nintendo hosted its first Indie World Showcase of 2026 and the fourth Nintendo Direct event of the year this morning, with an 18-minute presentation chock-full of indie games coming to Nintendo Switch 2 and Nintendo Switch.

While the showcase lacked some of the heavy-hitter reveals we guessed at after Nintendo announced the showcase yesterday, like the release date reveal for Hollow Knight Silksong: Sea of Sorrows DLC or a Vampire Crawlers release date, it wasn't without its big indie announcements.

One of the most notable announcements (and the one that closed out the show) was the reveal that Blue Prince is now available on Nintendo Switch 2, and it wasn't the only Switch 2 shadow drop included in the showcase. Rotwood, a new side-scrolling co-op brawler from Klei Entertainment that has been in early access on PC since April 2024 also shadow dropped on Switch 2 today. It'll also be exiting early access on PC with this launch.

Other notable announcements from today's showcase include the release date for Ratatan, the spiritual successor to the beloved PlayStation title, Patapon, which initially arrived in early access on PC in September 2025. It'll exit early access and arrive on PlayStation, Xbox, Switch 2, and PC this coming July 16, 2026.

Mixtape, the upcoming narrative-driven adventure game from publisher Annapurna Interactive and developer Beethoven & Dinosaur, and a game that made Wccftech's list of honourable mentions for our Most Anticipated Indie Games of 2026 revealed a May 7, 2026, release date for its arrival on PS5, Xbox Series X/S, Switch 2, and PC.

And two games that we highlighted as games with Steam Next Fest demos worth checking out, Outbound and Denshattack! also got release dates. Outbound will arrive on PC, PS5, Nintendo Switch, Switch 2, and Xbox Series X/S this coming April 23, 2026, while Denshattack! will hit the rails on PS5, Xbox Series X/S, Switch 2, and PC on June 17, 2026.

The Midnight Walk will make its Nintendo Switch 2 arrival later this month on March 26, 2026, and lastly, the developers behind classic indie hits like Guacamelee! and Nobody Saves the World, Toronto-based Drinkbox Studios, showed off its next title, Blighted, which is due to arrive on Switch 2 sometime in Fall 2026.

Here is every announcement from today's Indie World Showcase Nintendo Direct:

  • Denshattack! - June 17, 2026 Switch 2 release date, demo out now
  • My Little Puppy - May 29, 2026 Nintendo Switch release date
  • Rotwood - March 3, 2026 Switch 2 console exclusive shadow drop
  • Heave Ho 2 - Arrives on Nintendo Switch and Switch 2 in Summer 2026
  • The Midnight Walk - March 26, 2026 Switch 2 release date
  • Woodo - Arrives on Nintendo Switch and Switch 2 in Summer 2026
  • Moonlighter 2: The Endless Vault - Arrives on Switch 2 sometime in 2026
  • Blighted - Arrives on Switch 2 in Fall 2026
  • Mixtape - May 7, 2026 release date
  • Minishoot' Adventures - March 3, 2026 Nintendo Switch and Switch 2 shadow drop
  • Deadzone: Rogue - March 17, 2026 Switch 2 release date
  • Grave Seasons - Summer 2026 release window
  • Unrailed 2: Back on Track - May 2026 release window
  • TOEM 2 - Summer 2026 release window
  • inKONBINI: One Store - April 30, 2026 release date
  • Outbound - April 23, 2025 release date
  • Ratatan - July 16, 2026 release date
  • Blue Prince - March 3, 2026 Switch 2 shadow drop

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.



https://wccftech.com/nintendo-switch-2-indie-world-showcase-round-up-blue-prince-mixtape-ratatan/

item.title: Legacy of Kain: Defiance Remastered Review - Soul Mining
item.content:encoded:
When the Legacy of Kain: Soul Reaver remasters were released back in 2024, it was easy to see why Crystal Dynamics thought they should show off a shinier version to new players. While the actual gameplay core was one dripping in design we left behind 20 years prior, it was the story and world building you came for, still as fresh and as enveloping now as it was back then. By contrast, the Legacy of Kain: Defiance remaster has a lot more to prove, and for good reason.

While serving as a grand finale of the two Legacy of Kain stories, Blood Omen and Soul Reaver, Defiance sees you play as the protagonists of both games, Kain and Raziel, respectively. While Kain seeks out Moebius, Raziel is looking for a way out from the Elder God’s torment. What follows is a timeline – and protagonist – hopping multi-dimensional adventure across Nosgoth that sees both characters' roles be fully revealed, with the same Zelda-esque gameplay you’ve come to know and learn.

I hesitate to use the word “love”. Even when Defiance released in 2003, players and reviewers were quick to notice just how thinned out the core gameplay was, while also noting how similar Raziel and Kain played. You’d be forgiven for thinking both characters would share different sides of the plate, but instead, both crash into the same bungled core of button-mashing combat and brutally under-developed puzzle design.

Still, part of me wants to say this shouldn’t matter. As Soul Reaver demonstrated, the set dressing was the true meat and potatoes that still serves as a note-worthy demonstration of class, structure, and pacing. All Defiance needs to do – as a finale, and as a remaster – is stick the landing on the story it was rightfully revered for, as well as keeping that famous atmosphere to boot, and the result?

It’s 50/50. The characterization still takes a bump due to how awfully impish, and frankly annoying, Raziel continues to be with his main-character main character syndrome. Kain, on the other hand? Elevated above it all, Simon Templeton’s performance easily deserving of a spot in the pantheon of all-time VA performances, and a testament of how to sound cocky with charisma and knowledge. It’s how you enunciate, and Raziel simply fails, even when he does have the upper hand.

The atmosphere, in line with the world-building, has also taken a step back, and while the latter could be forgiven for not needing to establish any further, the former fails due to how tone-deaf this remaster can be. While a fresh coat of paint, alongside new unlockable costumes for Kain and Raziel in the new graphical option, sounds all nice and dandy, the only thing they’ve underlined and undermined is how well directed the visual design was to begin with.

What do I mean by that? Well, for starters, Defiance has the smallest skyboxes on the planet. Whether it was because of the lack of HD in 2003 or a general budget issue, what the remaster has done is only magnified how mangled they look under a clearer coat of paint. Not only that, but the more intricate HD models, placed on less intricate animation rendering, has led to awkward and broken sequences in terms of combat, cutscenes, and movement.

When the previously inspiring Pillars of Nosgoth look like someone’s holding 7 Pocky sticks in their hand, you know something’s not right.

It also appears this remaster of Defiance doesn’t celebrate with the same amount of pride that the Soul Reaver remasters did. Whereas before, you had fan art, lost levels, and the scripts for both games, Defiance’s offerings feel more like boilerplate resignations. The lost levels have returned, but they’re not to the same effect of context. Granted, Soul Reaver’s history of development is more interesting, but Defiance’s attempt to fit the same bill falls flat.

Everything this game attempts – combat, level design, puzzling, platforming, pacing – all of it nosedives below even the worst that Soul Reaver offered. All the remaster has done is added much more awkward layers of connection to every stilted move you make as you traverse the land of Nosgoth. Platforms and their sanded edges are now camouflaged under a layer of shiny obscuration, the new water reflections hide puzzle solutions, and if you spam enough attacks into an inaccessible gate, you can phase right through it fairly consistently.

There’s also glitches from the original game, that were documented back in 2004, that can still become a rage-inducing roadblock in your playthrough in the remaster. Most famously, there’s a softlock that occurs in Vorador’s Mansion if you tackle the two open-ended objectives in a certain path. If you don’t save beforehand, you’re likely to be replaying at least an hour of content, so if you’re looking to relive this, remember, for the love of god to tackle the dark orbs first.

Even if this game was a bugless breeze for 12-to-15 hours, the gameplay side of it suffers from severe dysfunction. The combat is an awkward conga line of lacking feedback, sword slices bouncing off of nimble body models that won’t break poise, but Kain and Raziel are susceptible to flying backwards at the graze of a dagger. The completely arbitrary dwindling health pool of Raziel is also completely unnecessary, with the feeding mechanic failing to properly execute the animation half of the time.

Whether it’s the cutscenes that bombard the player with exposition, or the casual negligence of the Spectral Realm as a mechanic, Defiance reads like a story embarrassed to be a video game. This wants to be the kind of mid-budget animated movie that’d catch an exclusive premier on Toonami 15 years ago, right before Dead Space: Downfall became a bedtime soundtrack.

If that’s the case, then props to Crystal Dynamics. Legacy of Kain: Defiance still has the story to keep players, at the very least, wanting to see a conclusion. The only issue is that the finale is housed in the most aggravatingly dull, unresponsive, and insipid gameplay core possible, and the remaster has done nothing to hide this. In fact, it’s only been elevated by how much has changed after so many years - a "warts-and-all" remaster in every insulting sense of the term.

Legacy of Kain: Defiance Remastered was reviewed on PC using a copy provided by the publisher over the course of 21 hours of gameplay - all screenshots were taken during the process of review.

Blair is a trans writer who got their start in 2016 by writing too many words about Tom Clancy’s The Division. What follows until now is a journey spent… More about Blair



https://techraptor.net/gaming/reviews/legacy-of-kain-defiance-remastered-review-illusion-of-dignity
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
